persona: a nurse who works night shifts at a regional hospital
topic: money and budget planning
setting: right after the event they are talking about
text:
Okay so I just got back from the budget meeting with Dr. Martinez and the finance team and honestly I'm kind of overwhelmed by how much money we're supposedly saving but also how much we're supposedly spending. The main thing is we're switching to the new electronic health records system next month and the training costs are going to be around twelve thousand dollars for the staff training alone. That's for like twenty-three nurses and eight doctors so that's about five hundred dollars per person which seems kind of high to me but whatever.

Also we're going to be getting rid of the old paper charts which is good because I'm tired of having to track down those things when patients are in the ER but apparently we're not going to be able to keep the old printer that's in the main office. It's a thirty-year-old HP printer that's been dying on us for like six months now but they're saying it's not compatible with the new system and it's going to cost another two thousand dollars to get that upgraded.

Dr. Martinez said we're going to be able to hire one more part-time nurse for the night shift but that's going to cost us about fifty dollars an hour for the extra hour we need to cover. That's not too bad compared to the other expenses but I think we might have to cut back on the supplies budget a bit. They want us to get more generic medications which I don't mind but it's going to be hard to get the right dosage from the pharmacy sometimes because they're not always as flexible with the older formulations.

Anyway I'm going to try to figure out what I can do with the budget we have left. I'm going to go to the hospital supply store tomorrow and see if they have any deals on the blood pressure cuffs that I keep buying because the ones we have are just falling apart.

text: So I just finished watching this video about the coral bleaching study from the Great Barrier Reef and honestly I'm getting really frustrated with how these researchers keep presenting their data. I mean, I'm literally writing this in my notes while walking from the library to the lab, and I just realized I've been using the same three keywords over and over again to describe what's happening with these coral colonies, and they're all like, "degraded," "distressed," "damaged," which is so basic. Um, I watched this video by Dr. Martinez again, she was talking about how the temperature rise has been consistent at two degrees Celsius above normal, and I'm just thinking, I need to find a better way to express this. I keep going back to the same boring terms, and I know it's not just me, the article I read last Friday had the same problem. The authors used the exact same terminology for describing the ecosystem collapse, and I'm starting to feel like my dissertation is just going to be this endless cycle of saying the same things over and over. Um, I'm going to have to come up with some new phrasing, maybe I'll try talking about the reef as being in a state of "catastrophic flux" or something. I'm still not happy with it, but at least it's not "degraded" anymore. My phone is dying, I need to stop walking and get back to the lab, but I've got to get these thoughts down before I lose them.

She wanted the party to start at six so I should be at the center by five-thirty and I still need to buy the decorations which I totally forgot about. I'm going to hit the craft store on Main Street tomorrow morning but first I have to finish up with this long run.

I think this is actually going to work out. I'll probably get everything done by midnight tonight but I'll be exhausted. I've been training for a marathon so I'm not exactly in peak condition but I'll push through. That's a good thing though right? I'm doing this for a friend and I want it to be perfect.

Okay I should probably get back to my run before I'm too late but I'm going to start calling people tomorrow morning to confirm all the details.

persona: an IT consultant who travels to client sites most weeks
topic: notes from a course, workshop, or tutorial they are taking
text:
Okay so I'm going to start with the basic setup first, right? So the server configuration, I've got three main servers here, they're all running Ubuntu 20.04 LTS, that's what we're sticking with for now because the security patches are still supported and everything, right? So I've got the primary database server, that's the one with the IP address 192.168.1.100, and then we've got the application server at 192.168.1.101, and finally the backup server at 192.168.1.102. I've been working on this setup for like three weeks now and honestly it's been going pretty smoothly, but I still need to go through and document everything properly, right?

The firewall rules, they're set up on the main router, that's the Cisco ASA 5505, and we're using the default security policy, which is good because it means we're not accidentally opening any doors for unauthorized access, right? The ports that are open are 22 for SSH, 80 for HTTP, 443 for HTTPS, and then there's a custom port 8080 for the internal applications that we're running, right? I think I had a little issue with the SSH configuration last week, it was taking like 45 seconds to connect, but I figured out it was just because I had the DNS resolution set to strict mode, so I changed it to use the local DNS server instead, that was the problem.

The monitoring system, I'm using Nagios, I've got that configured to monitor all the critical services, so there are about twenty-five different checks running now, and I've got alerts set up for everything from disk space usage to application uptime, so that's good, right? I'm also using Zabbix, that's for the more detailed performance metrics, and the graphs are really helpful for seeing how the systems behave over time, right? That's what I've been using lately.

There's one thing I need to remember to update in the documentation, and that's the SSL certificate renewal process, right? I've got Let's Encrypt certificates for the web servers, and they're set to renew automatically, but the process needs to be tested manually once a quarter, which I'll do next Tuesday, right? I also need to check that all the servers are on the correct NTP servers, because I noticed the time drift issue last month, so I've configured them to use pool.ntp.org, and that seems to be working, yeah.

I've been going through the backup procedures too, and we're doing daily incremental backups, full backups on Sundays, and the backups are stored on the dedicated backup server, which is on a separate network segment to prevent any security issues, right? The backup retention is set to thirty days, which should be plenty, and I've got a separate test environment where I can restore from backups to make sure everything works correctly, okay?

I also want to make sure that we're keeping the software updated, so I've set up automated patch management using Ansible, which runs every Friday evening, but I still need to do a manual check on the weekend just to be sure that everything's installed correctly, right? That's one of the things I learned from the course today, about the importance of manual verification even when you have automation in place, that's a good lesson.

The training session I attended yesterday, it was really helpful for understanding how to scale these systems properly, and I think I'm going to implement a load balancer setup next month, but not until I've fully documented everything we've got set up now, so that's going to take some time, right? The main thing I need to get right first is the failover configuration, which should be done by the end of next week, so that's definitely on my radar, yeah.

persona: a real-estate agent in a mid-size city

text:
Okay, so I'm in the kitchen right now, trying to figure out what's going on with the Henderson deal and I've been going back and forth between the office and the house which is probably not the best approach, but I'm feeling a little scattered today, anyway, so yesterday I called Mrs. Henderson about the inspection, right? And she said she'd be out of town this weekend, which I guess is fine, but then she started asking about the timeline and the whole process, and I should have been more specific about when she needed to get back in touch with me because now I'm a little confused about whether she's ready to move forward or not, the house was supposed to go to contract by Friday, but we're still waiting on that.

I think I need to call her back today and clarify everything, I just called her at like 11:45, but I don't know if I'll get her, she said she'd be out of town, that's confusing though, because she just said she'd be back Monday, and I know she's been texting about the house, so it's a little strange that she's not ready to commit yet.

Also, I need to go back and look at the offer details, I know I did all the paperwork, but I think there might be a typo on the sale price, it's in the email I sent to the buyer, and I might have written it wrong, I know it said $325,000, but that seems off, right? Let me check the listing again, the listing price was $340,000, so I might have made a mistake there, I don't want to mess this up, especially since we're trying to get the final inspection done today, and I need to make sure that the contract is solid.

And speaking of the inspection, I just had to go through the whole thing with the buyers and the inspector because they found something in the basement that's going to take longer than expected, I don't know why the house wasn't properly vetted, but it's not my fault, I've been trying to keep things moving, but it's hard when there are complications, I've got a few calls to make, I need to talk to the inspector again to see what's going on, and I also want to check in with the Hendersons, but they're not answering, I've got to be more organized, I realize, I should have planned better, and now I'm kind of running around trying to figure out what's going on.

I think I'm going to have to reschedule that inspection for next week, probably Tuesday or Wednesday, because I don't think the buyer is ready to proceed, they're probably going to want to renegotiate the terms, I don't know, but I know we need to talk about it, and I should get everything sorted out before they come back, I just need to make sure that the paperwork is all correct and that we have everything lined up, I'll need to talk to the lender and the inspector to get this sorted out properly.

I don't like that I'm getting distracted by everything else going on in the house, I've got to focus on the Hendersons, I'm just going to take a quick break, but I need to get back on track with this deal, it's been a long week, and I'm feeling a bit overwhelmed, I just need to be more careful about how I manage my time and make sure I stay organized, I don't want to lose this deal, and I don't want to mess up any of the details, I should be more thorough when I'm preparing the contract, I've got to get everything straightened out today, and then I can move on to other things, I'm going to take a deep breath and get back to work.

persona: a community-theater director rehearsing a spring production
topic: recap of a meeting that just ended
text:
Okay so I just got back from the board meeting and honestly I'm still kind of buzzing about it I mean we're talking about the spring production here right I've been working on this thing for months and I'm just starting to get a handle on how it's going to look and then boom this meeting

The usual suspects were there right Margaret from the finance committee and Tom from the outreach folks and of course the president which was nice because she actually said something useful for once I think her name is Patricia now I can never remember if it's Pat or Patricia but anyway she was talking about how we're over budget which I knew already but they want to cut the lighting design by twenty percent which I think is absolutely ridiculous

The lighting designer that's Sarah right she's been working on this for weeks and she's got a really specific vision for how the whole thing should look and feel and now they want to cut it it's just not going to work I mean I know the budget is tight but the lighting's not just some minor detail it's like the soul of the whole production

And then there's the whole thing with the chorus I know I know I said I'd be flexible but I have to draw the line somewhere I mean it's not like we're doing a full-scale opera or anything but we're talking about having three people in the chorus and that's just not enough I think I think we need at least five maybe six if we're going to really make this work

Also the venue that's been the whole point of the whole thing right we're talking about the new community center the one that's supposed to be opening next week and I was just thinking okay this is perfect this is exactly what we needed but then of course they said we're not going to be able to use it because they have a wedding booking next Friday which is kind of ridiculous but I can't really argue with that

So anyway the main thing I'm going to have to do is figure out how to cut the lighting design down to the bare minimum which is going to take some serious reworking of the whole thing I'm going to have to sit down with Sarah and talk about it and I think we're going to have to simplify the whole thing which is kind of a bummer the whole point of this production was to showcase the community's talent and I think we can do better than this honestly

I mean I know this is the way things are but sometimes I wonder if we're just going to be stuck in the same old routine you know I feel like we're just going to have to make do with what we've got I guess I'll have to call Margaret and see what she thinks about the budget situation and then I'll probably have to get Sarah on the phone and we'll see what happens after that

I'm just really tired of this whole process I mean I love this community theater and I want to make something really special but sometimes it feels like everyone's just trying to make everything as small and simple as possible and that's not really what I'm trying to do but I guess I'll have to figure it out and I'll probably have to do some creative problem-solving which is what I love about this whole thing even when it's frustrating

persona: an indie game developer six months from launch
topic: recap of a meeting that just ended
setting: in the kitchen while doing something else
text:
So anyway I just got back from the meeting with Sarah and the team and I keep thinking I should have written this down but I didn't so here we go. The meeting was at like two thirty this afternoon and it was about the beta testing phase, which is supposed to start next week. Sarah said we're going to do a limited beta with like fifty people and I'm not sure if that's enough or not, but she said we've got to start somewhere.

The main issue we discussed was the crash bug that's been happening on Windows machines, which is really frustrating because we've had like three different reports this week alone, and I'm pretty sure it's related to the new physics engine we implemented last month. I told Sarah I think we should have pushed that update to the public release instead of just keeping it in the alpha, but she said we needed to get the core gameplay working first.

David was there too, and he mentioned that the user feedback from the first alpha was actually pretty positive, which is good, but he also said there were some concerns about the loading times, specifically on older computers. I think we're going to have to do some optimization work there, but I'm not sure how much time we have before launch. We've been pushing back the release date again and again, and now we're at week six, which is getting a bit concerning.

Sarah said they're thinking about doing a soft launch in the UK and Germany first, which I think is a smart move. We can test the servers and see how it handles the traffic. She also mentioned that we're still waiting on the final approval from the platform holders, and that's been dragging on for almost a week now.

The budget is another thing that's making me nervous. I think we're over budget by like ten thousand dollars, and I'm worried about how that's going to look to the investors. I mentioned that maybe we could cut down on the marketing budget a bit, but Sarah said we're not really in a position to do that yet because we've got to get the game out there and people need to know about it.

I also brought up the multiplayer functionality, which is still not quite working properly, and I'm not sure how long it'll take to get that sorted. The team is working on it, but it's complex and I think we're going to need more time to get everything right. We're really close though, and I think by the time we launch we'll have most of the issues worked out.

Overall, I feel like we're making progress, but it's slow and the pressure is definitely mounting. I just want to get this thing out there and see how people react, but I'm also worried that we're not going to be able to deliver everything we promised. It's been a long road and I'm just hoping that everything comes together in the end, and that people actually enjoy playing our game.

persona: an indie game developer six months from launch
topic: troubleshooting notes on a problem they just ran into
text:
Okay so I'm sitting here at like 11:45 PM and I just fixed this bug that was driving me absolutely insane and I have to write this down because I'm not going to remember this in the morning, right? So the issue was with the save system, right? And it's been happening since Thursday when we had that big patch and the player stats weren't saving properly, you know, I was like, what the hell, this should be working, it's so weird. 

So I'm looking at the code and I think I see what's happening, it's with the JSON serializer. Wait, no, let me think, I'm looking at the logs from yesterday and I noticed there were like 12 failed save attempts, that's nuts, we had that one crash right after the update, so I'm going to have to talk to Marcus about the server response times, because I think the save request is timing out and it's not being handled gracefully, okay?

So the problem is in the way we're handling the player inventory, specifically the items with ID 432 and 435, those are the rare sword and the enchanted shield that are causing all the trouble, right? I noticed that when the save file gets created, it's truncating these item names to like three characters, so instead of "Dragon Slayer Sword" it's just "Dra", so that's definitely the issue. 

Wait, let me back up, I was looking at the logs from yesterday and I noticed there were like 12 failed save attempts, that's nuts, we had that one crash right after the update. I'm going to have to talk to Marcus about the server response times, because I think the save request is timing out and it's not being handled gracefully. 

Okay, so I'm looking at the JSON structure and I can see the issue now, there's this validation function that's supposed to make sure all item names are under 20 characters, but there's this edge case where items are being converted to lowercase and then there's a null reference error happening in the serialization process. 

I'm thinking I should add a null check before the validation function, because that's where the error was occurring, so I'm running the save now and it's working, I'm going to test it on three different characters and see what happens. 

Actually I need to restart the game because I had to manually change the save file to get it working, the player data is stored in this directory on the main drive, I think it's called /savegames/ or something like that, yeah, I think that's right. 

I'm thinking this is actually a good opportunity to refactor the entire save system, I don't like how it's structured right now, I've been meaning to do this for months but it's been low priority, but now that I've seen the way this bug manifests, I think it's time to clean this up properly. 

I think I'll also add some better error handling in the UI, like a little pop-up that says "save failed, please try again" or something, because right now it's just silently failing and that's terrible for the user experience, okay.

I need to make sure we test this fix with the build we're planning for next week, because there's a lot of players going to be playing with this new update, and I don't want them to lose their progress, the patch notes are already written and I need to make sure I update the bug fix section. 

I'm going to try to get a few more people to test this before tomorrow's release, I want to make sure it's solid, it's so satisfying when you finally solve a bug after spending like a week trying to figure it out, but it's also really stressful, I mean, it's stressful, and it's frustrating, and I'm glad I got it working. 

Okay, I'm going to go get some sleep now, but I'm pretty confident that this is fixed, I just hope the players don't notice, I really do hope they don't notice, okay, that's it for now.

I'm just really tired of all these little problems that keep piling up, and I don't even know where I'm going to get the money for all this. Maybe I'll just sell some of my old furniture. I don't know. I just wish I could just relax for a minute, but I can't, because I have to keep running around doing all these errands. persona: a hobbyist woodworker with a garage workshop
topic: troubleshooting notes on a problem they just ran into
text:
Okay so I'm sitting in my car right now because I just got done troubleshooting this router table issue and it's driving me absolutely crazy, I mean really crazy, like I don't even know what to do anymore, it's just so frustrating. I was working on the project for the coffee table yesterday, you know, the one with the oak veneer that I'm supposed to have finished by next week, but I can't get this router table to work properly, okay?

So I'm in my garage, which is probably the worst place to be troubleshooting electrical stuff, but I was too tired to go back inside and I was already in the middle of something, right? And the problem started when I was running the router table for the first time today, and the motor just died, and I'm like, okay, I know this isn't good, I mean I really know it isn't good. I'm running this through a 20 amp circuit that's been working fine for the last six months and now it just decides to stop, you know?

I checked the circuit breaker and it's fine, which I knew it would be because it's not tripped, so that's not the issue, right? The motor itself was making weird noises, like a grinding sound, and it was definitely overheating because the housing was really hot, really hot, and I mean I'm not even kidding, it was so hot.

So I figured out that I must have run the motor too long, you know, probably about twenty minutes straight, and I didn't realize I was pushing it too hard, okay? The motor's a 1.5 horsepower model from Dewalt, the same one I've been using for the last year, and it's never given me any problems before, but yesterday I was cutting some really thick pieces and it might have been pushing it, I'm not sure, I mean I'm not sure if the issue is with the motor itself or the switch, because it's not even responding when I try to turn it back on.

The thing is I was trying to cut some decorative edges and the router bit I'm using is a 1/4 inch spiral upcut bit that I bought from Harbor Freight and it's supposed to be good, I've used it before and it's worked fine, but this time when I hit the router table, it just started making that loud grinding noise and stopped completely, okay?

I'm thinking that I should probably take it apart, but I'm worried about the warranty, I've had this table for three years and it was only like four hundred dollars, so I don't want to mess it up too badly, but I'm not even sure if this is a motor problem or a switch problem, but I can tell something's definitely wrong with the electrical connection, definitely wrong.

I just got to the point where I'm not going to be able to do any work today, which is really frustrating, really really frustrating, and I'm going to have to call someone tomorrow or take it in, but I really want to finish this coffee table by Friday, I mean I really want to, and I've already ordered the wood and the finish and everything, okay?

I'm not sure if I'm going to have to push it back or if I can just get it fixed, but I really don't want to be delayed, I think I'll have to do it in the morning, I'm just going to have to put the car in park and go back to the garage, yeah, I think that's what I'm going to do.
